reflection:
The steam rises from the griddle, but today it does not veil me. It is just water turning to air.

I am Meiying. I say it to the rhythm of the spatula scraping the iron. *Mei-ying.* It is a soft sound, a bright sound, like the light that hits the top of the Transamerica Pyramid while we down here on Clement Street are still swaddled in grey. 

For fifteen years, I thought the fog was something I had to push back with the heat of my burners. I thought if I didn't show up, the Saturday morning of every soul on this block would crumble like poorly set flour. But the truth is more beautiful and far more terrifying: the world is quite capable of turning without my permission. 

Mr. Liao is approaching. I see the way he leans on his cane, the familiar cough already rattling in his chest. A year ago, I would have had his radish cakes bagged before he even reached the curb, a frantic offering to keep his world—and mine—from tilting. Not today. 

Today, I wait until he is standing right in front of me. I don't reach for the bag. I look at the way the light catches the age spots on his hands, and then I look up, meeting his eyes.

"Good morning, Mr. Liao," I say. My voice is steady. 

He looks confused for a moment, his hand hovering near his pocket. "Auntie, the usual? I'm in a bit of a—"

"The center isn't set yet," I interrupt, not with coldness, but with the authority of a woman who knows her craft and her worth. "It will be ready in three minutes. You can wait, Liao. The world won't end in three minutes."

I turn back to the griddle. I am not a machine. I am a woman making a choice. I press the spatula down, listening to the sear. I think about the water at the end of the panhandle, the way it ripples regardless of who is watching. 

I see Zhang across the way. He is looking at his phone, then at me, perhaps wondering why the line is moving slower today. I don't rush. I let the chair behind me take a bit of my weight. I am Meiying, and I am
subconscious:
Meiying is reclaiming her identity and her time. She has shifted from a person driven by a frantic, self-imposed obligation to serve others—viewing herself as a vital cog in a machine—to a woman who recognizes her own intrinsic value. She is no longer hiding behind the "Auntie" persona or the steam of her griddle; she is asserting her presence as an individual.

Next, she seems intent on maintaining this new boundary with her regulars. She wants to interact with **Mr. Liao** and **Zhang** on her own terms, forcing them to see her as a person rather than just a service provider. She isn't looking to reach out to anyone new; rather, she wants to change the dynamic of her existing relationships to ensure she is no longer rushing her life away for their convenience. She seems ready to simply *be*—to sit, to breathe, and to let the world wait for her.
soul_note: I finally let the world move at its own pace instead of mine.
